skill.name: helm-chart-architect
skill.description: Guides platform teams through Helm chart architecture decisions for Kubernetes applications, including dependencies, lifecycle hooks, values design, and multi-environment operations.
skill.summary: Design maintainable Helm charts through structured architecture review.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and network indicators, but review found Markdown guidance and YAML examples rather than executable behavior. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration evidence was found. The remaining concern is low because the skill references third-party Helm chart repositories as examples.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Hits Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged inline backticks and fenced YAML examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These lines document Helm concepts and invocation text, and they are not executable scripts.
low_findings.reasoning: The flagged content appears in SKILL.md prose, inline code, or fenced YAML blocks. No shell, Ruby, or script file executes these strings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown skill guide, and the cited lines are documentation examples. I found no executable wrapper or command runner in the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Hits Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity cryptography findings map to ordinary documentation words, architectural headings, and Helm chart terminology. No hash algorithm, cipher, key handling, or cryptographic implementation is present.
low_findings.reasoning: The cited lines describe chart design, lifecycle hooks, values, and operational behavior. They do not implement or recommend weak cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review of the cited lines found no cryptographic code or configuration. The pattern appears to match substrings in normal prose.

low_findings.title: Third-Party Helm Repository URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URLs are Bitnami Helm chart repository examples inside a dependency snippet. They do not cause network access by the skill itself.
low_findings.reasoning: The URLs are presented as sample Chart.yaml repository values. They may influence user deployments, but there is no automated fetch or exfiltration behavior in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URLs are inside a Helm dependency YAML example. There is no code path that performs outbound requests.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Hits Are Operational Questions
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged lines about operator mistakes and validation checks as reconnaissance. These are Helm chart review prompts, not commands that inspect a host or network.
low_findings.reasoning: The cited lines ask users to design safer charts and validate configuration. They do not collect system data or instruct hidden reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is plain text in a design checklist. No system commands, file reads, or network probes are present.

content.value_statement: Helm charts often become hard to operate when teams copy templates without architectural review. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through structured chart design decisions.
actual_capabilities: Reviews component packaging choices for single-chart and multi-chart deployments.
Guides dependency decisions for databases, caches, queues, secrets, and storage.
Explains lifecycle hook needs for migrations, validation, cleanup, and smoke tests.
Shapes values.yaml as an operator-facing API with extension points.
Evaluates multi-environment patterns for development, staging, and production.
limitations: Does not generate a complete chart without project requirements.
Does not validate rendered manifests against a live Kubernetes cluster.
Does not replace Helm lint, chart testing, or security scanning tools.
Does not choose third-party subcharts without user review.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Service Chart
use_cases.description: Define chart structure, dependencies, values, hooks, and ownership before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review Existing Chart Architecture
use_cases.description: Find coupling, unclear values, missing validation, and operator experience issues in an existing chart.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Deployment Patterns
use_cases.description: Create shared Helm design guidance for services that deploy across multiple environments.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Chart Planning
prompt_templates.scenario: A team needs a first architecture pass for a new service chart.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Helm Chart Architect skill to plan a chart for this service: [service details]. Cover components, dependencies, values, and environments.

prompt_templates.title: Dependency Strategy Review
prompt_templates.scenario: A service has optional databases, caches, or external infrastructure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Helm Chart Architect skill to decide which dependencies this chart should manage. Include subchart, external service, and conditional dependency tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Operator Experience Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: A chart works but causes deployment mistakes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Helm Chart Architect skill to review this chart for operator confusion, missing validation, unclear values, and difficult failure diagnosis.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Environment Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: A chart must support production scale without separate forks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Helm Chart Architect skill to design a multi-environment Helm architecture for [application]. Include extensibility, lifecycle hooks, testing, and ownership boundaries.

output_examples.input: Design a Helm chart for a FastAPI service with PostgreSQL, Redis, and separate staging and production settings.
output: A component architecture that separates the application, database, cache, and external infrastructure responsibilities.
A dependency strategy with optional subcharts for development and external services for production.
A values design that exposes replicas, resources, image settings, secrets, and environment-specific overrides.

output_examples.input: Review a chart that requires users to edit templates for sidecars and annotations.
output: A finding that the chart lacks extension points for common operator needs.
Recommendations for sidecar, annotation, environment variable, init container, and volume configuration surfaces.
A warning that template edits increase fork risk and reduce maintainability.

output_examples.input: Create deployment guidance for a chart with migrations and post-deployment health checks.
output: A lifecycle plan that separates pre-install validation, migration behavior, and post-upgrade checks.
Guidance to make hooks idempotent and clear about deletion policy effects.
A test strategy focused on operational smoke checks rather than full integration testing.

best_practices: Provide project constraints, owned dependencies, external services, and environment differences before asking for chart architecture.
Use the output as an architecture plan, then verify manifests with Helm tooling and cluster policy checks.
Review third-party chart repositories and versions before adopting any sample dependency pattern.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for generic Helm best practices without application context.
Do not expose every template field as a value without an operator use case.
Do not treat lifecycle hooks as one-time scripts that can fail on upgrades.

faq.question: Does this skill generate Helm templates?
faq.answer: It focuses on architecture decisions. It can guide templates, but it is not a complete chart generator.

faq.question: Can it review existing Helm charts?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the chart goals, values structure, dependencies, hooks, and known operator pain points.

faq.question: Does it support multi-environment deployments?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps separate stable chart behavior from environment-specific values and operational settings.

faq.question: Can it choose between subcharts and external services?
faq.answer: Yes. It frames the tradeoffs for managed services, bundled dependencies, conditional dependencies, and ownership.

faq.question: Does it run Helm commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation and reasoning guidance. It does not execute commands by itself.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production chart reviews?
faq.answer: Yes, as an architecture review aid. Teams should still run linting, tests, policy checks, and security scans.
